---
title: "Plant growth: a linear mixed-effects model"
subtitle: "AI-in-science workshop — worked analysis"
author: "Your name"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: show
    embed-resources: true
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  warning: false
  message: false
---

<!--
This is a STARTING SKELETON. Two ways to use it:
  (a) ask the Copilot agent to fill in / rewrite each section below, or
  (b) delete everything under the YAML header and ask the agent to write the
      whole document from scratch.
The headings below are just a suggested shape — change them freely.
Render with:  quarto render analysis.qmd   (or the Quarto extension's "Render")
-->

## What we're doing and why a mixed model

<!-- Explain, for a reader who has never used mixed models:
     - what the data are (link to data/README.md);
     - what a *random effect* is, in plain language;
     - why repeated measurements on the same plant aren't independent, so we need
       a random intercept (and slope) per plant;
     - how that differs from the *fixed* effects (week, genotype) we care about
       on average. -->

## The data

```{r}
#| label: load-data
library(tidyverse)
plants <- readr::read_csv("data/plant_growth.csv")
glimpse(plants)
```

<!-- A figure of the raw data showing the group structure: e.g. height vs week,
     one line per plant, coloured/facetted by genotype. -->

```{r}
#| label: fig-raw
#| fig-cap: "Plant height over time, one line per plant, by genotype."
# ... your ggplot here ...
```

## Fitting the model

```{r}
#| label: fit
library(lme4)
# m <- lmer(height_cm ~ week * genotype + (week | plant_id), data = plants)
# summary(m)
```

<!-- A *tidy* summary of the model (e.g. broom.mixed::tidy(m)); a sentence or two
     interpreting the fixed effects (do genotypes differ in growth rate?) and the
     random-effect SDs (how much do plants vary?). -->

## Looking at the fit

<!-- A figure of the fitted/partial effects — e.g. predicted growth trajectories
     by genotype with uncertainty (marginaleffects / ggeffects), or observed vs.
     fitted, or the per-plant random slopes. -->

```{r}
#| label: fig-effects
#| fig-cap: "Fitted growth trajectories by genotype."
# ... your ggplot here ...
```

## Validation: simulate from the fitted model and recover

<!-- The point of this section: convince yourself (and the reader) the fit is
     trustworthy. One good approach:
       1. simulate a new response from the fitted model (simulate(m));
       2. refit the same model to the simulated data;
       3. check the key parameters (fixed effects, variance components) come
          back close to the originals.
     Bonus: also fit the model a second way (e.g. glmmTMB) and check the
     estimates agree. -->

```{r}
#| label: validation
# ... your simulate-and-recover check here ...
```

## Takeaways

<!-- 3–5 bullets: what we learned about the plants, and what the modelling choices
     (random intercept + slope, the genotype interaction) actually bought us. -->
